text: The benefits of a low anterior abdominal incision have been previously demonstrated, including enhanced postoperative recovery and reduced postoperative pain. The reduced risk of respiratory-related complications can be achieved due to minimal disruptions on accessory respiratory muscles. Within the domains of robotic urological surgery, there has been limited evidence of using the incision for robotic retroperitoneal partial nephrectomy due to concerns surrounding distance to the kidney and the limited working space within the retroperitoneum. The purpose-built SPA robotic platform possess two prominent features, including the narrow profile and double articulating instruments. Both features allow for improvement of durability, especially in a small surgical working space which provide the opportunity for surgeons to regionalize surgery to the relevant anatomy.

The video shows a retroperitoneal robotic partial nephrectomy on a 69-year-old man with a left upper pole renal mass. The patient is positioned laterally with the operating table flexed. A 3.5 cm incision is made near the pubic tubercle, lateral to the midline. The external oblique fascia is incised, followed by blunt dissection to separate underlying muscles. The surgeon uses a finger to gently push the peritoneum away, creating space for the SP access kit insertion. The single-port robot is docked at an acute angle towards the target anatomy.

Upon entry into the retroperitoneum, the psoas major muscle is clearly visible. The surgeon performs dissections to expose the ureter as it crosses the iliac artery, continuing proximally towards the left kidney. The rotous fascia is opened, and dissections proceed towards the renal hilum, revealing the proximal ureter, renal artery, and renal vein. Two renal arteries and one renal vein are visible. Dissections continue to expose the upper pole renal mass within the gyrotous fascia with minimal fat excision. An interpretive ultrasound is performed to identify and mark the tumor.

Robotic bulldog clamps are placed on the two renal arteries and the single renal vein. The upper pole tumor is excised under ischemia with maintained hemostasis. Rhinorafi is performed in two layers using V-lock sutures, secured with hemo-lock clips using a sliding cleave technique. The clamps are removed after a total ischemia time of twenty-five minutes.

The surgeon places the excised tumor into an endocatch bag and removes it through the single incision. The robotic system is undocked, and the port is removed along with the intact specimen. The surgical site is closed in layers, and the skin is sutured. The procedure concludes with no intraoperative complications.

The patient was reviewed one week following his surgery where he remained pain-free, and examination demonstrated good healing of the left lower abdominal incision site. Our initial series consists of four patients with a median age of sixty-eight years and a median BMI of thirty-five point four. Three patients had previous abdominal surgery, one of which had more than five previous surgeries. There were no interpretive and postoperative complications in our series. The median ischemia time was twenty-four minutes and negative surgical margin was achieved in all four cases. Our initial series demonstrated the favourable perioperative outcomes with all patients being discharged within twenty-four hours and with none requiring opioid analgesia on discharge.

Our initial experience demonstrated that robotic retroperitoneal partial nephrectomy for any tumor location can be safely performed through a low anterior abdominal incision using the purpose-built SP platform. Perioperative outcomes in our series were favorable with reduced length of stay and postoperative analgesia requirement while maintaining favorable oncological and functional outcomes.
